items.title: Riotous Assemblies (Scotland) Act 1822
items.summary: Amends prior UK laws to streamline and cheapen recovery of damages up to £30 caused by riotous assemblies or malicious acts against property (churches, homes, mills, factories, mines, agricultural assets, etc.) in England and Scotland. Introduces summary proceedings via magistrates' petty sessions in England and sheriff courts/small debt courts in Scotland, with collective liability on local inhabitants, towns, or counties to compensate victims quickly, reducing legal costs over full court actions.
items.reason: This Act protects private property rights by ensuring efficient compensation for riot damage, preventing unrecoverable losses that would distort investment incentives, raise insurance costs, and deter economic activity in potentially unstable areas. Collective local liability internalizes the costs of disorder, encouraging communities to maintain order without relying on distant or ineffective central authorities. Deleting it would leave small-scale victims worse off, as prior full-court processes were cost-prohibitive (costs often exceeding damages), leading to under-protection of property, reduced prosperity, and weakened incentives for liberty-preserving institutions; modern equivalents exist but this foundational mechanism aligns with prioritizing property over mob action.

items.title: Church Building Act 1822 (repealed)
items.summary: 1822 Act amending prior legislation to facilitate building additional churches in populous parishes by enabling land donations from public bodies, loans and rates for construction, compulsory land acquisition, parish divisions, endowments, pew rentals, and administrative provisions for ecclesiastical infrastructure.
items.reason: This Act empowers government commissioners with coercive tools (land grants without compensation in some cases, compulsory purchase, loans charged on local church rates akin to taxes) to subsidize and direct Church of England expansion, distorting property markets, imposing fiscal burdens on parishioners, and favoring one religion via state intervention. Unseen costs include reduced incentives for private/voluntary church building, bureaucratic overhead, and perpetuation of established church privileges amid modern secularism; private charity and market provision would better serve religious needs without coercion.
